coredata.dc:description: Porphyrin-based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with efficient bactericidal performance have increasingly attracted attention in photodynamic inactivation materials. However, low reactive oxygen species (ROS) yield and drug residue hazards of current porphyrin-MOFs materials lead to unsatisfactory clinical therapeutic effects. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were encapsulated into PCN-224, which enhanced the photodynamic activity of the MOFs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Singlet oxygen (1O2) detection confirmed that the photodynamic activity of CQDs-doped PCN-224 (CQDs@PCN-224) was enhanced than that of pristine PCN-224 under illumination. Furthermore, the CQDs@PCN-224 were firmly embedded into bacterial cellulose (BC) nanofibrous membranes by using an eco-friendly biosynthetic approach, efficiently preventing MOFs leakage during use. The results of bactericidal assays demonstrated that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane with higher photodynamic activity causes more severe disruption to bacterial structure and possesses better antibacterial efficiency (>99.99 % reduction of both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli O157:H7 within 30 min) than BC/PCN-224 membrane under visible light illumination (500 W, 15 cm height, λ ≥ 420 nm). In addition, the biosynthesized BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane showed good hemocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, revealing that the BC- and MOFs-based material with enhanced PDI efficiency and satisfying safety has great potential in medical fields.

head.ce:title: Photodynamic activity enhanced by in situ biosynthetic BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes through FRET strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porphyrin-based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with efficient bactericidal performance have increasingly attracted attention in photodynamic inactivation materials. However, low reactive oxygen species (ROS) yield and drug residue hazards of current porphyrin-MOFs materials lead to unsatisfactory clinical therapeutic effects. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were encapsulated into PCN-224, which enhanced the photodynamic activity of the MOFs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Singlet oxygen (1O2) detection confirmed that the photodynamic activity of CQDs-doped PCN-224 (CQDs@PCN-224) was enhanced than that of pristine PCN-224 under illumination. Furthermore, the CQDs@PCN-224 were firmly embedded into bacterial cellulose (BC) nanofibrous membranes by using an eco-friendly biosynthetic approach, efficiently preventing MOFs leakage during use. The results of bactericidal assays demonstrated that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane with higher photodynamic activity causes more severe disruption to bacterial structure and possesses better antibacterial efficiency (>99.99 % reduction of both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli O157:H7 within 30 min) than BC/PCN-224 membrane under visible light illumination (500 W, 15 cm height, λ ≥ 420 nm). In addition, the biosynthesized BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane showed good hemocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, revealing that the BC- and MOFs-based material with enhanced PDI efficiency and satisfying safety has great potential in medical fields.

The nano-granular MOFs were firmly sandwiched into BC membrane with enhanced photodynamic activity via FRET.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: For a long time, the infection problem caused by microbes has posed a serious threat to people and resulted in huge economic losses on a globe scale (Chen et al., 2020; Cihanoglu & Altinkaya, 2020; Ma et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). Bacterial infections usually occur after skin injuries, especially without appropriate sterilization management, which can lead to non-functioning scar tissue formation and chronic wounds during wound healing (Kharaziha et al., 2021). To avoid the problem, many kinds of antibiotics have been developed and widely used in a large dose, but lead to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria and ineffectiveness against superbugs (Yu et al., 2020). Therefore, the efforts to vigorously develop safe, effective, and non-antibiotic anti-infective agents are urgently needed (Nguyen & Lai, 2022). Over the past few decades, reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based photodynamic inactivation (PDI) has been proposed as a novel antibacterial mechanism to kill bacterial. PDI relies on the photochemical reactions between photosensitizers and light to rapidly generate ROS from ambient molecular oxygen, which can severely damage bacterial cells and ultimately lead to bacterial death (Castro-Alferez et al., 2017; Rahman et al., 2018). Especially, PDI is a nonspecific and non-triggered method that effectively inactivate multiple types of pathogens and is unlikely to make pathogens resistant (Gamelas et al., 2022).

In recent years, nanosized porphyrin-based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have been widely used for PDI as an emerging class of porous antibacterial agent (Peng et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2020). In particular, PCN-224 MOFs, formed by the linkage between the Zr6 and the TCPP ligand (TCPP = tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)-porphyrin), have good biocompatibility, chemical stability, and efficient diffusion of ROS (1O2) under visible radiation, which have showed tremendous application potential in biomedicine (Jiang et al., 2022; Jin et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). Although these researches have confirmed many advantages of PCN-224 for PDI, we proposed that the 1O2 generation of the MOFs could be further increased to enhance the PDI efficiency. For example, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was a versatile strategy to enhance 1O2 generation (Li et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019). FRET is defined as a non-radiative energy transfer process between an excited-state donor molecule and a ground-state acceptor molecule, however, it only occurs when the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the distance between the donor and acceptor is <10 nm (Lindsey et al., 2021). Based on these two basic principles, we proposed to use fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as excited-state donors for PCN-224 acceptor to achieve FRET process. CQDs are one of the zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with remarkably advantages in their green synthesis, good chemical stability, low toxicity, low-costs, and strong light trapping ability (Ali et al., 2020; Li, Li, et al., 2021). CQDs have drawn considerable attention in a wide range of applications, such as bioimaging, catalysis, sensing, antibacterial agents, and optoelectronic device due to their promising properties (Jian et al., 2020; Jian et al., 2017; Jin et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2017). Recently, some composite materials based on the synergistic effect of CQDs and MOFs have been fabricated and reported, such as CQDs@ZIF-8, C60@PCN-222 and CQDs@UiO-66 (Si et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2019). However, most of these researches have focused on catalysis, and few studies have reported their antibacterial applications. Inspired by these researches that focused exclusively on the photocatalysis, we speculated that the CQDs with specific fluorescence emission could be embedded into porous PCN-224 to enhance the PDI efficiency via the FRET process.

On the other hand, most of the traditional approaches to employ porphyrin-MOFs are unsatisfactory due to the leakage and residue of the agents are not consideration, and some reports of MOFs-based photodynamic therapy are even suspected on account of lacking a specific method of administration (Ding et al., 2022; Han et al., 2020; Li, Song, et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2021). Thus, finding an ideal material or method to carry these photosensitizers is a crucial issue for developing their applications in medical field. To solve the problem, in situ growth strategy was conducted to fabricate cotton/PCN-224 fabrics for MOFs carrying, nonetheless, the use of high temperatures and hazardous organic solvents during the production process could still lead to the high industrial energy consumption and high risk to human health (Nie, Wu, Huang, Wang, & Wei, 2021; Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, Li, & Wei, 2020; Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, & Wei, 2020). Meanwhile, our group has reported a vacuum-assisted filtration method to immobilize photosensitive MOFs (PCN-224 nanoparticles) on bacterial cellulous (BC) (Nie, Wu, Huang, Li, et al., 2021). However, carrying MOFs by such method meant that the low loading of PCN-224 nanoparticles and inevitably cause the nanoparticles leakage during use. Fortunately, BC has turned out to be an ideal immobilization carrier for nanomaterials, as it can be combined with various types of chemical additives in the culture medium to fabricate flexible and biocompatible nanocomposites (Sarkandi et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2022). Furthermore, based on the high optical transparency of wet BC membranes, we surmised that the in situ biosynthesized BC carrier could provide a favorable light excitation environment for the incorporated photosensitive MOFs.

In this study, for the first time, we developed a novel in situ biosynthesized composite with enhanced PDI activity through FRET strategy. First, the fluorescent CQDs were selected as FRET donors and further introduced into porous PCN-224 nanoparticles by solvothermal method to construct FRET pair compounds (termed CQDs@PCN-224). The morphology and chemical structures of the CQDs@PCN-224 were systematically characterized, in addition, a series of spectroscopy characterization and 1O2 detection were employed to verify the enhancement of photodynamic activity and the feasibility of FRET process. Subsequently, the CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles were immobilized by a one-step in situ biosynthetic process to fabricate a tightly bonded photosensitizer-BC nanocomposite (termed BC/CQDs@PCN-224). The morphological and structural characterizations could confirm that the CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles were firmly sandwiched between BC nanofibrous membranes and were unlikely to cause leakage during use. Furthermore, we evaluated the bactericidal performance of the biosynthesized BC/CQDs@PCN-224 against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli O157:H7 under a Xenon Lamp illumination, and the morphology of these killed bacteria was observed by SEM. To confirm the satisfying biocompatible of BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane, rabbit red blood cells and mouse fibroblast cells were employed to investigate its hemocompatibility and cytotoxicity, respectively. All of our findings revealed that the biosynthesized BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes with high PDI activity and excellent safety could be used as a promising medical material for infection control.

ZrOCl2·8H2O (99.9 %), 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP, 97 %), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMPONE), and 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. The fresh rabbit blood was obtained from Qingdao Antiserum Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China. Mouse fibroblast cells were provided by Medical School of Nantong University. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, glutaraldehyde (50 %), benzoic acid (99.5 %), citric acid (CA), 1,2-ethylenediamine, and other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. All the chemicals and regents were analytical grade.

CQDs were synthesized by a facial green one-step microwave method according to previous report with slightly modification (Zhai et al., 2014). As shown in Scheme. S1, in detail, 1 g of CA was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and then 0.8 mL of ethylenediamine was added evenly to the solution with stirring. The mixed solution was heated in a microwave oven (700 W) for 3 min to obtained brown colloids. After that, the brown colloids were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 30 min) for 3 times to collect the clear and yellow brown supernatant liquor. Finally, the yellow brown aqueous solution was freeze dried to obtain the CQDs.

PCN-224 were synthesized according to a previous method (Nie, Wu, Hussain, & Wei, 2021; Peng et al., 2019). In detail, 2.8 g benzoic acid, 0.1 g TCPP and 0.3 g ZrOCl2·8H2O were dissolved in 100 mL DMF under ultrasonication. The mixture was then heated in an oil bath at 90 °C for 6 h with mild stirring. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the product (PCN-224) was collected by a centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 30 min) and washed with DMF and ethanol 5 times to remove unreacted chemicals. Finally, PCN-224 nanoparticles were dried in vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h and stored in dark place.

1.4 g benzoic acid, 50 mg TCPP and 150 mg ZrOCl2·8H2O were dissolved in a flask containing 50 mL DMF, then, 2 mg of CQDs were added to the flask and sonicated for 10 min. After that, the flask containing homogeneous solution was heated in an oil bath at 90 °C for 6 h, and cooled to ambient temperature after reaction. The products were centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 30 min) and washed with ethanol for 5 times, dried in vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h. The obtained nanoparticles were denoted as CQDs@PCN-224 and kept in dark place.

Gluconacetobacter xylinus (ATCC 10245) was used for in situ biosynthetic process (Shen et al., 2021). The process is exhibited in Scheme. 1 . In detail, the synthesized MOFs (PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224) and BC medium (2.5 % mannitol, w/v, 0.3 % yeast extract, w/v, and 0.5 % peptone, w/v) were separately sterilized at 121 °C for 17 min. After sterilization, the MOFs were uniformly dispersed in the BC medium by sonication at a concentration of 2 mg/mL. In the dark, 0.9 mL of the MOFs/medium mixture was mixed with 0.1 mL of G. xylinus starter culture and incubated in a 24-well plate at 30 °C. Initially, the MOFs would form precipitate in the mixed culture solution, and the BC fibrils would grow at the air-liquid interface. After 4 days of static incubation, the upper layer of cellulose fibrils could bind the lower layer of MOFs precipitate to form initial BC/MOFs composites, at this time, the BC/MOFs composites in each well were inverted under sterile and dark conditions and cultured for another 4 days to further firmly bind the MOFs. Afterwards, the obtained BC/MOFs hydrogels were soaked in 1 % sodium hydroxide solution at room temperature for 12 h, then washed with deionized water for 5 times. Finally, these hydrogels were freeze-dried to obtain BC/MOFs hybrid membranes, including BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224, respectively.

The morphologies of PCN-224, CQDs@PCN-224, BC, BC/CQDs@PCN-224 were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Regulus8100, HITACHI, Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU1510, HITACHI, Japan). The average diameters and size distributions were measured by using Nano Measurer (Fudan University, China). The morphologies of CQDs and CQDs@PCN-224 were observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, HITACHI, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were recorded by a NEXUS 470 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was characterized by a thermal analyzer at a heating rate of 20 °C/min (TGA2, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were recorded by an EDAX Octane EDS-30 System (AMETEK, USA). The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D2 PHASER, BRUKER, Germany), and the scanning angle was ranged from 2° to 40° with a step size of 2°/min. The UV–visible spectra were measured by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). The N2 adsorption-desorption behaviors and pore size distribution were measured by a fully automatic four-station specific surface pore size analyzer (Autosorb iQ Station 1, Quantachrome, USA), the samples were heated at 100 °C under N2 atmosphere for 6 h before testing. A dynamic contact angle measuring instrument (DCAT21, Dataphysics, Germany) was used to record the water contact angle. The mechanical property was evaluated by using a tensile tester (INSTRON 1185, Instron, USA) with 5 mm measuring distance at a steady speed of 20 mm/min at 25 °C, and the relative humidity was maintained at around 40 % during the test.

The ability of PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 to produce 1O2 was investigated by electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR, Bruker EMXplus-10/12, Germany) using TEMPONE as a capture agent. Briefly, the MOFs nanoparticles were dispersed in deionized water to obtain 0.1 mg/mL homogeneous MOFs suspension, then the suspension was mixed with 100 mM TEMPONE/H2O solution in equal proportion and exposed to a Xenon Lamp (500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, 15 cm) for 30 min. The 1O2 generation was detected by the ESR spectroscopy. In addition, 1O2 generation of PCN-224, CQDs@PCN-224, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 were also measured by using DPBF under 520 nm irradiation. For MOFs, PCN-224 or CQDs@PCN-224 were uniformly dispersed in absolute methanol at a concentration of 2.5 mg/mL. 5 μL of the PCN-224 or CQDs@PCN-224 suspension was added in 1.5 mL DPBF/methanol (20 ppm) and mixed homogeneously at room temperature, then exposed to illumination for 1 min and recorded by UV–visible spectrophotometer every 10 s from 350 to 500 nm. For BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 composite membranes, a piece of the sample (10 mm × 10 mm) was added in the bottom of a quartz cuvette, then 3.5 mL of absolute DMF containing DPBF (20 ppm) was added. After that, the cuvette was exposed to illumination for 1 min and recorded every 10 s.

The stability of BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes was performed according to previous studies to assess the hazards caused by MOFs leakage (Ruiz et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2019). Briefly, all the samples were dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h before the test. Then, the dried samples were weighed and put into PBS solution under ultrasonication for 10 min. After that, the treated samples were dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h until constant weight. Besides, the samples under the two treatments: soaking and stirring, respectively, in PBS solution for 24 h were also investigated. The residual weight (%) was calculated according to Eq. (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial efficiency and SEM images of bacterial morphology
ce:section.ce:para: The biocidal efficacy of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 samples against Gram-positive S. aureus (ATCC 6538) and Gram-negative E. coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43895) were evaluated by a modified AATCC 100-2004 Test (Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, Li, & Wei, 2020; Wang, Ying, et al., 2021). In detail, the square samples (10 × 10 mm) were respectively placed on the bottom of a sterile 24-well plate, and 25 μL of bacterial suspension was inoculated on each sample. Then, the 24-well plate was transferred to a Xenon Lamp (500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, 15 cm) for 15 min and 30 min illumination. In contrast, the same samples were tested in another 24-well plate under darkness. Afterwards, 1 mL PBS was added to each well and vortexed for 30 s to suspend bacteria, then the bacterial suspension was diluted serially 3 times (10, 100, 1000-fold) and 50 μL of the dilution was evenly dropped on agar plates. After incubation at 37 °C for 16–24 h, the bacterial colonies on agar plates were counted. All the biocidal experiments were carried out in triplicate.

To investigate the effect of ROS on bacterial structure, the morphology of the bacteria was observed by SEM. After bacterial suspension was inoculated on samples and exposed to light for 15 min, the samples were soaked in 2.5 % (w/w) glutaraldehyde solution at 4 °C for 24 h and gradually dehydrated in alcohol solution with different concentrations (50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 100 %). Finally, the dehydrated samples were dried at 37 °C before test.

The hemolytic activities of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 samples were tested according to a reported method to evaluate their hemocompatibility (Peng et al., 2021). In detail, 5 mL of fresh rabbit blood and 10 mL of PBS were homogeneously mixed, then the mixture was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min to collect the red blood cells (RBCs), diluted in PBS to prepare 5 % (v/v) RBCs/PBS solution. 1 mL of the 5 % RBCs/PBS solution was mixed with 4 mL of sample/PBS suspension (10 mg/mL) and in incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, meanwhile, the 5 % RBCs/PBS solution was mixed with PBS and deionized water as negative and positive controls, respectively. After incubation, the mixtures were centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatants were collected and measured at 540 nm by a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The hemolysis ratio (%) was calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
Hemolysis ratio
%
=

× 100
where A
sample , A
negative , and A
positive represent the absorbance of supernatants collected from samples, PBS, and deionized water, respectively.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 samples was tested by using mouse fibroblast cells (L929) according to CCK-8 assay method (Ozkan et al., 2020). L929 cells were incubated in DMEM medium under 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for resuscitation, then seeded in 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Meanwhile, a sterile circular sample (Ф = 1.5 cm) was incubated in DMEM medium at 37 °C for 24 h to obtain sample extracts. After that, the culture medium in 96-well plate was slowly sucked out and replaced with the same volume of sample extracts, subsequently incubated for another 24 h. The absorbance of the cell culture medium was measured at 540 nm after adding CCK-8 reagent. L929 cells incubated in DMEM medium without sample extracts were used as a positive control.

The live and dead L929 cells were observed by using live-dead fluorescent staining. Briefly, the cells in each well were washed with PBS to remove the excess solution. After that, the cells were stained with diluted Calcein-AM and incubated in the dark for 15 min, then washed with PBS for three times and stained with diluted PI and incubated in the dark for 5 min. Finally, the stained cells were washed and observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope.

The experimental data were presented as mean ± standard deviation, and the ANOVA and Student's t-test were used for statistical analysis. The differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 1a, the schematic diagram illustrates the fabrication process and the structure of CQDs-doped PCN-224 (CQDs@PCN-224). PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 were collectively termed as MOFs in the following. This diagram exhibits a porous structure of PCN-224 nanoparticles, which consists of a Zr6 cluster with D3d symmetry and a TCPP ligand. Meanwhile, the pre-synthesized CQDs were encapsulated into the PCN-224 nanoparticles during the solvothermal reaction process. The prepared CQDs, PCN-224, and CQDs@PCN-224 were systematically characterized. As shown Fig. 1b and c, the FESEM images show that the synthesized PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 were nearly spherical crystals, and that the average diameters were 91.2 ± 8.8 nm and 99.1 ± 18.1 nm, respectively. In Fig. 1d, the TEM image showed that the CQDs were distributed in the range of 0.8 to 3.1 nm with an average diameter of 1.6 ± 0.4 nm. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1e, a lot of CQDs (marked by red arrows) could be observed inside rather than outside of CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles, meanwhile, the partially magnified inset in this picture presented the CQDs more clearly, confirming the successful encapsulation of CQDs in PCN-224. The UV–visible absorption property of PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224, as well as the photoluminescence (PL) property of CQDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. 1f, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of PCN-224 exhibited a strong peak at around 440 nm, besides, another four characteristic peaks were observed in the range of 500 to 700 nm. On the other hand, the PL spectrum of CQDs showed maximum emission around 450 nm, more significantly, the fluorescence emission ranging from 400 to 600 nm overlapped with the UV–visible absorption spectrum of PCN-224, providing the feasibility for the occurrence of FRET. After introducing CQDs into PCN-224, the CQDs@PCN-224 showed five similar absorption peaks to PCN-224 in the range of 400 to 700 nm, while a strong peak could be observed around 360 nm which was attributed to the CQDs absorption (Zhai et al., 2014). In addition, the photograph images (Fig. S1) also presented the intense fluorescent emission of CQDs under UV light illumination. Hence, for CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticle, the encapsulated CQDs can be used as excited-state donors for PCN-224 acceptor to ensure the occurrence of FRET phenomenon.

As shown in Fig. 1g, the physical structure of the synthesized CQDs, PCN-224, and CQDs@PCN-224 were further characterized by XRD. The pattern of CQDs displayed a broad diffraction peak located at around 19.5°, indicating their amorphous graphite structure (Xia et al., 2021; Zhai et al., 2014). The pattern of PCN-224 exhibited four sharp peaks at around 4.8°, 6.2°, 8.1°, and 10.9°, manifesting the successful synthesis of the PCN-224 (Feng et al., 2013; Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, & Wei, 2020). After CQDs introduced, the pattern of CQDs@PCN-224 showed the diffraction peaks consistent with PCN-224 at around 4.8°, 6.2°, 8.1°, and 10.9°, meanwhile, a weak and broad diffraction peak at around 15–20° could be observed, which might be attributed to the introduced CQDs. The chemical structures of these products were analyzed by FTIR (Fig. S4). As exhibited in CQDs spectrum, the characteristic peaks at 2935 cm−1 and 1435 cm−1 were ascribed to CH bonds. The broad band from 3500 cm−1 to 3200 cm−1 suggested the presence of NH and -OH bonds, and the peaks located at 1650 cm−1 and 1198 cm−1 belonged to the amide bond absorption peaks. The strong peak of the skeletal vibrations from benzene rings was observed at around 1570 cm−1. In addition, CO and C-OH stretching could be observed at 1740 cm−1 and 1200–1000 cm−1, respectively (Xia et al., 2021; Zhai et al., 2014). For PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 spectra, the CO stretching vibration and CN bending vibration could be observed at 1700 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1, respectively (Nie, Wu, Hussain, & Wei, 2021). The characteristic peaks of CQDs were not obvious in CQDs@PCN-224 spectrum, which could be attributed to the low doping of CQDs. In addition, the spectrum of CQDs@PCN-224 was similar to that of pristine PCN-224, which could confirm that the chemical structures of CQDs@PCN-224 remain unchanged after doping CQDs. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 at the temperature of 77 K were conducted to certify their pore structure (Fig. S5). PCN-224 displayed a typical type I isotherm without hysteresis, suggesting the crystal and microporous nature (Jin et al., 2021). Whereas, after encapsulating the CQDs into PCN-224, the curve of CQDs@PCN-224 changed to a type IV isotherm and showed a lower N2 adsorption capacity, indicating the existence of mesopores and reduced porosity, respectively (Xia et al., 2021). Combined with the analysis of pore size distribution (Fig. S6), it can be speculated that the partially micropores (<2 nm) in CQDs@PCN-224 are occupied by CQDs, however, the introduction of CQDs may lead to the formation of mesopores (2–2.5 nm) during MOFs growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical characterization and FRET analysis of CQDs@PCN-224
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the visible absorption, fluorescence, and phosphorescence properties of the synthesized MOFs, the same concentrations (0.5 mg/mL) of PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 were homogeneously dispersed in ethanol and characterized by corresponding instruments. The visible absorption spectra of the MOFs/ethanol suspensions are exhibited in Fig. 2a. The spectrum of PCN-224 displayed a maximum absorption peak at 440 nm and four weak peaks in the range of 500 to 700 nm, which was consistent with the results in Fig. 1f. With the introduction of CQDs into PCN-224, the absorbance of CQDs@PCN-224 was improved over the whole visible range (400–700 nm), confirming the enhanced light-harvesting capability of the MOFs. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of the MOFs were recorded at 440 nm excitation to study the FRET effect. As shown in Fig. 2b (left), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@PCN-224 at 660 nm was higher than that of PCN-224, indicating that the energy transfer between encapsulated CQDs and PCN-224 was successfully occurred. Meanwhile, in the phosphorescence spectra (right), because of the CQDs encapsulation, the spectrum of CQDs@PCN-224 displayed a stronger peak at 465 nm than that of PCN-224, which was related to the enhanced generation process of 1O2 (DeRosa & Crutchley, 2002). And this phenomenon will be further explained in the following.

DPBF was chosen for 1O2 detection since the chemical could specifically react with the generated 1O2 and formed colorless o-dibenzoylbenzene, leading to an absorbance decrease at around 415 nm (Carloni et al., 1993). As displayed in Fig. 2c, after exposure to 520 nm illumination, the absorbance in CQDs@PCN-224 pattern (0.462 decrease) decreased more than that in PCN-224 pattern (0.266 decrease) within 1 min, suggesting that CQDs@PCN-224 could generate more 1O2 in the same time. Furthermore, the other two control groups (including: MOFs mixed with DPBF and kept in dark; MOFs alone exposed to illumination) were also performed within 1 min (Fig. 2d and e). As shown in the two control groups, the absorbance spectra of DPBF mixed with MOFs in dark (Fig. 2d) and the absorbance spectra of MOFs under illumination remained unchanged with time increasing (Fig. 2d), revealing that the decrease in absorbance of DPBF was indeed due to the 1O2 produced by MOFs under illumination. According to the inference, the rate of decrease in absorbance can be used to represent the rate of 1O2 generation. As exhibited in Fig. S7, the control groups showed four corresponding fitting curves with slopes close to 0, indicating that 1O2 was not produced during these control experiments. Whereas, two fitting curves with −3.94 × 10−3 slope and −5.95 × 10−3, were observed in Fig. S7, which belonged to PCN-224 and CQDs@PCN-224 experimental group, respectively. The result revealed that the 1O2 generation rate of CQDs@PCN-224 was about 1.51-fold increase than that of PCN-224, which was attributed to the introduction of CQDs. To further confirm this conclusion, ESR measurements were also employed for 1O2 detection. As shown in Fig. 2f, CQDs@PCN-224 displayed a stronger signal than PCN-224, indicating the stronger ability to produce 1O2, which was consistent with the results by using DPBF as the detector.

According to the above analyses, the FRET mechanism for enhanced 1O2 generation can be illuminated. As displayed in Fig. 2d, apart from the inherent photodynamic activity of PCN-224, the incorporated CQDs (donor) can absorb light energy and further transfer to PCN-224 (acceptor) to improve the photodynamic efficiency. In detail, under the visible light, the donor absorbs irradiation energy of appropriate wavelength and jumps from ground state to an excited singlet state. Generally, the donor in the excited singlet state is unstable and easily jumps back to its ground state with fluorescence emission. Herein, due to the FRET phenomenon, the resonance energy of the fluorescent donor will be non-radiatively transferred to the acceptor through dipole-dipole interactions, and further stimulates acceptor to the excited triplet state (Imani et al., 2021; Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, & Wei, 2020; Wang, Liu, et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019). The acceptor in the excited triplet state will react with triplet oxygen (3O2) to produce biocidal 1O2 and emit phosphorescence, and jump back to its ground state eventually (DeRosa & Crutchley, 2002; Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, Li, & Wei, 2020).

As mentioned earlier, finding an ideal material or method to homogeneously carry MOFs is a crucial issue for developing their applications in medical field. However, currently most approaches may easily lead to MOFs leakage or affect the PDI efficiency of MOFs during clinical applications. Hence, a in situ immobilization strategy is utilized to anchored the MOFs into BC nanofibrous network via an eco-friendly biosynthetic process (Shen et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 3a, the FESEM image showed the typical 3D nanoscale network structure of freeze-dried BC/MOFs, which consist of BC nanofibers with an average diameter of 47.3 ± 12.0 nm. And the inset in this FESEM image exhibited the BC/MOFs membranes in wet condition. Obviously, the high optical transparency of wet BC membranes provided convenience for light absorption of the embedded MOFs. To further observe the interior structure of BC/MOFs membranes, the cross-section of the nanocomposite was characterized by SEM (Fig. 3b). This image exhibited a sandwich-like structure of BC/MOFs membrane, in which the MOFs were compactly encapsulated in interlayer by BC nanofibers on both sides. By using a higher magnification (Fig. 3c), the image showed that the encapsulated MOFs were tightly bonded by BC nanofibers, further confirming the firm structure of the BC/MOFs membranes. The compact sandwich-like structure of BC/MOFs membrane was formed by its inversion during the cultivation process (Nandgaonkar et al., 2014). EDS analysis was also performed on the cross-section of BC/MOFs membrane to confirm its inherent sandwich-like structure. As displayed in Fig. 3d, four elements including C, O, N, and Zr were detected and their spot signals were presented in four mappings, respectively. The spot signals of C and O formed two well-defined outer-layers in the mappings, while the signals from N and Zr were mainly distributed at the interlayer of BC/MOFs membrane. In addition, the EDS spectra (Fig. 3e) presented the signal peaks of C, O, N, and Zr at 0.26 keV, 2.38 keV, 0.52 keV, and 2.12 keV, respectively, and the contents of corresponding element were exhibited in Fig. 3f.

The biosynthesized BC and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane were characterized by FTIR, XRD, and TGA. In FTIR spectra (Fig. S8), the characteristic peaks of pristine BC membrane at around 3340 cm−1, 2890 cm−1, and 1050 cm−1 were attributed to -OH, -CH2, and C-O-C stretching vibrations (Shen et al., 2021). All characteristic peaks of BC could be observed in BC/CQDs@PCN-224 spectra, however, the peculiar band at 1600–1510 cm−1 only appeared in BC/CQDs@PCN-224 spectra, which was ascribed to the skeletal vibrations of benzene rings from embedded CQDs@PCN-224. XRD analysis (Fig. S9) was used to confirm the physical structure of the encapsulated CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles in BC nanofibrous layers. As shown in BC pattern, three diffraction peaks of cellulose I crystalline structure were found at around 2θ = 14.8°, 16.8°, and 22.9°, which were assigned to (1−10), (110), and (220) crystallographic planes, respectively (Castro et al., 2012; Niu et al., 2019). The characteristic peaks of cellulose could be observed in BC/CQDs@PCN-224 pattern. In addition, new diffraction peaks belonging to BC/CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles (dotted circle) were also found in this pattern, indicating that the physical structure of CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles has not changed. TGA (Fig. S10a) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG, Fig. S10b) were performed to investigate the thermal decomposition behavior of the biosynthesized BC and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes. Obviously, the BC/CQDs@PCN-224 exhibited a greater amount of charred residue than BC, which was attributed to the incomplete thermal decomposition of introduced MOFs. And the DTG curves showed the decomposition temperature of the MOFs was around 400 °C, corresponding to the previous report (Feng et al., 2013). Significantly, both BC and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes exhibited excellent thermal stability to 265 °C, indicating that such BC- and MOFs-based materials can be suitable for practical applications.

Previous researches have demonstrated that the higher hydrophilicity of material surface plays a significant part in PDI efficiency (Dong et al., 2018). To evaluate the hydrophilicity of BC and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes, the infiltration behavior within 10 s of water droplets (5 μL) on the membranes was recorded. As shown in Fig. S11, both BC and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes possessed good hydrophilicity. However, the hydrophilicity of BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane was decreased in comparison with BC membrane, which can be speculated that the hydrophobic CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles below the BC layer obstruct the rapid diffusion of water. The mechanical performance of the obtained BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes was also evaluated by a tensile testing machine. From Fig. 3g, the stress and elongation at break of BC membranes decreased after encapsulating CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles. This may be due to the granular CQDs@PCN-224 in the interlayer cannot provide effective intensity like BC nanofibers. Although the test results reveal that the encapsulation of CQDs@PCN-224 in BC membrane leads to decrease of mechanical properties, BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes possessing 12.5 MPa breaking stress and 27 % breaking elongation will satisfy most practice applications (Wang, Hou, et al., 2021). Preventing the leakage of CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles from BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes is an outstanding performance for the in situ biosynthesized materials. Therefore, we further evaluated the fastness of the embedded nanoparticles into BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes. As exhibited in Fig. 3h, the BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes displayed <1.5 % leaching after the three special water bath treatments, confirming that CQDs@PCN-224 nanoparticles were tightly anchored into BC membranes. In addition, 1O2 generation of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes was detected to investigate their photodynamic activity. As displayed in Fig. S12, the absorption spectra of BC showed no change within 1 min owing to no photosensitive MOFs loaded. Whereas, BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 showed 0.176 decrease and 0.310 decrease at 415 nm within 1 min, respectively. Hence, it can be inferred that 1O2 produced by MOFs instead of BC, besides, the BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes possess stronger photodynamic activity than the BC/PCN-224 membranes. Meanwhile, the corresponding fitting curves (Fig. 3i) exhibited that the photodynamic efficiency of BC/CQDs@PCN-224 was 1.68-fold greater than that of BC/PCN-224, further demonstrating the feasibility of FRET strategy for photodynamic enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and biocompatibility analyses of BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial efficiency of biosynthesized BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 were performed to investigate their PDI activities. Herein, all experimental groups were tested in the dark for 30 min and under illumination for 15 or 30 min. The initial bacterial suspensions directly tested in the dark and under illumination for 30 min were used as control groups.

As exhibited in Fig. 4a and b, the antibacterial efficiency of control groups suggested that the effect of visible light illumination on the PDI results was negligible. The pure BC membranes displayed the reduction of 5.01 % (dark) and 23.75 % (light) against S. aureus and 19.12 % (dark) and 21.15 % (light) against E. coli O157:H7, respectively, which was mainly ascribe to the bacterial adhesion instead of inactivation and could be regarded no significant antibacterial activity (Wang et al., 2022). As expected, the BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes showed no statistically significant efficiency against S. aureus or E. coli O157:H7 in the dark, indicating that the two MOFs-embedded BC membranes did not possess biocidal activity in the absence of illumination. However, under the visible light illumination, both the BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes showed gratifying antibacterial performance. After 15 min exposure, BC/PCN-224 membranes showed the reduction of 94.50 % and 90.88 % against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7, respectively. Notably, for 15 min exposure under light, BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes exhibited a stronger PDI activity against S. aureus (99.45 % reduction) and E. coli O157:H7 (99.03 % reduction). This enhancement phenomenon could also be observed from the antibacterial data of BC/PCN-224 (S. aureus: 99.65 % reduction, E. coli O157:H7: 99.29 % reduction) and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 (S. aureus: >99.99 % reduction, E. coli O157:H7: >99.99 % reduction) after exposure under light for 30 min. Fig. S13 displayed the survival colonies of S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 after the PDI testing, it could be obviously that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes possessed stronger PDI activity against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 than BC/PCN-224 membranes. These results confirmed the broad-spectrum PDI against bacteria under reasonable illumination using biosynthesized BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 as biocidal materials. Furthermore, the enhanced antibacterial efficiency revealed that the FRET strategy consisting of Zr-based porphyrinic MOFs (PCN-224) and CQDs achieves a higher photodynamic activity against bacteria.

In order to further elaborate the inactivation mechanism against bacteria, the morphologies of S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 after contacted with BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes and exposed to visible light illumination for 15 min were observed by SEM (Fig. 4c). On BC membranes, both S. aureus (spherical) and E. coli O157:H7 (rod-shaped) displayed typical cell morphologies with intact cell surfaces, indicating that both bacteria were not damaged by BC (Han et al., 2020; Schnaider et al., 2017). In contrast, the bacteria with deformed structure could be observed on BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes, which caused by the oxidative destruction of 1O2 produced from the embedded MOFs (Chen et al., 2019). Such oxidative disruption to bacterial cellular membranes abnormally increased the membrane permeability, resulting in leakage of intracellular fluid and bacterial death eventually. Significantly, after exposed to light for a same time, the bacteria on BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes were more thoroughly destructed than those on BC/PCN-224 membranes, also confirming the enhanced PDI activity of BC/CQDs@PCN-224. This analysis was in agreement with the results of 1O2 detection and antibacterial efficiency, demonstrating that the high-efficient production of 1O2 plays a crucial role in causing damage to bacterial structure.

To evaluate the compatibility of the BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membranes in biomedical applications, rabbit red blood cells and L929 cells were employed to test the hemocompatibility and cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 5a, the hemolysis rates of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 samples were all below the red dash line (5 %), which was considered to be an acceptable limit for hemocompatibility (Wang et al., 2022). The inset in Fig. 5a exhibited the photographs corresponding to the hemolysis test, obviously, the positive control (deionized water) showed a transparent red solution caused by the rupture and hemolysis of RBCs in deionized water. Whereas, the supernatants of experimental groups (BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224) were almost colorless, similar to the negative control (PBS). Fig. 5b shows the cell viability of the cytotoxicity assay, as illustrated, the rates of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 were 98 ± 7 %, 93 ± 2 %, and 88 ± 2 %, respectively. In addition, live-dead cell fluorescence staining was conducted to observe the morphologies of L929 cells after co-cultured with the extracts of BC, BC/PCN-224, and BC/CQDs@PCN-224. As shown in Fig. 5c, the live cells were stained green exhibited oval or spindle like morphologies in all groups, indicating these cells were in a healthy status. However, a few dead cells stained red could be observed in BC/PCN-224 and BC/CQDs@PCN-224 groups, confirming their low cytotoxicity to L929 cells. Nevertheless, the two PDI materials with satisfying cell viability (93 ± 2 % and 88 ± 2 %, respectively) also could be accepted (Nie, Wu, Mensah, Wang, Huang, Li, & Wei, 2020). As a whole, the assessments on the hemolysis and cytotoxicity confirm that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane possesses good biocompatibility and could be utilized in medical field such as wound management.

In this research, the CQDs with strong fluorescence emission at 400–600 nm were selected as energy donors and successfully doped into the PCN-224 acceptor to construct the FRET pairs. Meanwhile, the 1O2 detection test confirmed that the photodynamic activity of CQDs@PCN-224 was significantly enhanced through the FRET process. To further develop an ideal application strategy, the synthesized CQDs@PCN-224 were immobilized into BC membrane using an eco-friendly in situ biosynthetic approach. These nano-granular CQDs@PCN-224 were firmly embedded into BC membrane as an interlayer with <1.5 % leaching after three specific water bath treatments. Importantly, the high optical transparency of wet BC nanofibers and their high hydrophilicity could provide facilitations for light transmission and 1O2 transport. The result of antibacterial efficacy demonstrated that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 could kill >99.99 % of both S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 under visible light for 30 min, while BC/PCN-224 caused 99.65 % and 99.29 % reduction against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7, respectively. Furthermore, the assessments of the hemocompatibility and cytotoxicity confirmed that BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane possessed good biocompatibility. Therefore, all these results indicated that the BC/CQDs@PCN-224 could be utilized in medical field, such as clinical therapeutic of bacteria-infected wounds.
